---
title: "Module 1: Signals"
subtitle: "What Electrical Signals from the Body Look Like"
format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
---

::: {.clinical-vignette}
A 28-year-old woman comes to your clinic with episodes of "staring off" lasting 10–15 seconds,
happening several times a day. Her partner says she doesn't respond during these episodes.
You order an EEG. The technician hands you a tracing. It looks like this:
:::

```{=html}
<div class="widget-box" style="background:#f8f9fa;">
  <div id="clinicalEEG" style="width:100%; height:250px;"></div>
  <div style="font-size:0.85em; color:#777; text-align:center; margin-top:4px;">
    Simulated EEG showing rhythmic 3 Hz spike-and-wave discharges — the hallmark of an absence seizure.
  </div>
</div>

<script src="https://cdn.plot.ly/plotly-2.35.2.min.js"></script>
<script>
(function() {
  // 3 Hz spike-and-wave: the classic absence seizure pattern
  function seededRand(s) { return function() { s=(s*16807)%2147483647; return (s-1)/2147483646; }; }
  const rand = seededRand(99);
  const sr = 1000, dur = 5;
  const t = [], y = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < dur * sr; i++) {
    const ti = i / sr;
    t.push(ti);
    const phase = (ti * 3) % 1; // 3 Hz cycle
    let v = 0;

    // Normal background (first 1s and last 0.5s)
    if (ti < 1.0 || ti > 4.5) {
      v = 25 * Math.sin(2*Math.PI*10*ti) + 10 * Math.sin(2*Math.PI*7*ti)
        + 8 * (rand()-0.5);
    } else {
      // Spike-and-wave pattern
      // Sharp spike (~30ms)
      if (phase < 0.09) {
        v = 120 * Math.sin(Math.PI * phase / 0.09);
      }
      // Slow wave
      else if (phase < 0.7) {
        v = -60 * Math.sin(Math.PI * (phase - 0.09) / 0.61);
      }
      // Return to baseline
      else {
        v = 10 * Math.sin(2*Math.PI*10*ti);
      }
      v += 6 * (rand()-0.5);
    }
    y.push(v);
  }

  const trace = {
    x: t, y: y, type: 'scatter', mode: 'lines',
    line: {color: '#2c3e50', width: 1.3},
    hovertemplate: 'Time: %{x:.2f} s<br>%{y:.0f} μV<extra></extra>'
  };

  // Shade the seizure region
  const layout = {
    xaxis: {title: 'Time (seconds)', range: [0, dur]},
    yaxis: {title: 'μV', range: [-100, 160]},
    margin: {t: 10, b: 45, l: 50, r: 15},
    plot_bgcolor: '#f8f9fa', paper_bgcolor: '#f8f9fa',
    shapes: [{
      type: 'rect', x0: 1.0, x1: 4.5, y0: -100, y1: 160,
      fillcolor: 'rgba(231,76,60,0.08)', line: {width: 0}
    }],
    annotations: [
      {x: 0.5, y: 150, text: 'Normal', showarrow: false,
       font: {size: 11, color: '#27ae60'}},
      {x: 2.75, y: 150, text: 'Seizure (3 Hz spike-and-wave)', showarrow: false,
       font: {size: 11, color: '#c0392b'}},
      {x: 4.75, y: 150, text: 'Normal', showarrow: false,
       font: {size: 11, color: '#27ae60'}}
    ],
    hovermode: 'x unified'
  };

  Plotly.newPlot('clinicalEEG', [trace], layout, {responsive: true, displayModeBar: false});
})();
</script>
```

Can you tell what's happening? When does the abnormal activity start? What's different about it?

By the end of this tutorial, you'll be able to answer those questions — and understand *why* the signal looks the way it does. But first, we need to learn the language that describes any electrical signal from the body.

## The Basics

Every cell in your body produces tiny electrical signals. Neurons fire. Heart muscle contracts. Skeletal muscle twitches. These are not abstract physics — these are **your patient's physiology**, translated into voltage over time.

A signal is just a measurement that changes over time. When we record an EEG, ECG, or EMG, we're measuring **voltage** (in microvolts or millivolts) at each moment.

The simplest signal you can imagine is a smooth, repeating wave. It has two properties that matter right now:

- **Amplitude**: How tall the wave is. Bigger amplitude = stronger signal.
- **Frequency**: How fast the wave repeats. Higher frequency = more cycles per second.

That's it. Two knobs. Let's play with them.

## Interactive: Build a Signal

Drag the sliders. Watch the wave change in real time.

```{=html}
<div class="widget-box">
  <div style="margin-bottom: 0.8em;">
    <label style="font-weight:600; font-size:0.9em; color:#555;">Try a clinical preset:</label><br>
    <button class="preset-btn" onclick="setPreset(10, 5, 'Alpha rhythm — eyes closed, relaxed')"
            style="padding:6px 14px; margin:3px; border:2px solid #8e44ad; background:#8e44ad; color:white; border-radius:20px; cursor:pointer; font-size:0.85em;">
      Alpha (10 Hz)
    </button>
    <button class="preset-btn" onclick="setPreset(2, 8, 'Delta rhythm — deep sleep (real amplitude ~150 μV)')"
            style="padding:6px 14px; margin:3px; border:2px solid #2c3e50; background:#2c3e50; color:white; border-radius:20px; cursor:pointer; font-size:0.85em;">
      Delta (2 Hz)
    </button>
    <button class="preset-btn" onclick="setPreset(20, 3, 'Beta rhythm — focused attention')"
            style="padding:6px 14px; margin:3px; border:2px solid #e67e22; background:#e67e22; color:white; border-radius:20px; cursor:pointer; font-size:0.85em;">
      Beta (20 Hz)
    </button>
    <button class="preset-btn" onclick="showECGPreset()"
            style="padding:6px 14px; margin:3px; border:2px solid #27ae60; background:#27ae60; color:white; border-radius:20px; cursor:pointer; font-size:0.85em;">
      Heart (~72 bpm)
    </button>
  </div>
  <div id="presetLabel" style="font-size:0.88em; color:#8e44ad; font-style:italic; margin-bottom:0.6em; min-height:1.2em;"></div>
  <div style="margin-bottom: 1em;">
    <label for="ampSlider" style="font-weight:600;">Amplitude (μV):
      <span id="ampValue" style="color:#e74c3c; font-weight:700;">5.0</span>
    </label><br>
    <input type="range" id="ampSlider" min="0.5" max="10" step="0.1" value="5"
           style="width:100%; accent-color:#2980b9;">
  </div>
  <div style="margin-bottom: 1em;">
    <label for="freqSlider" style="font-weight:600;">Frequency (Hz):
      <span id="freqValue" style="color:#27ae60; font-weight:700;">10.0</span>
    </label><br>
    <input type="range" id="freqSlider" min="0.5" max="30" step="0.5" value="10"
           style="width:100%; accent-color:#2980b9;">
  </div>
  <div id="signalPlot" style="width:100%; height:320px;"></div>
</div>

<script>
(function() {
  const ampSlider = document.getElementById('ampSlider');
  const freqSlider = document.getElementById('freqSlider');
  const ampValue = document.getElementById('ampValue');
  const freqValue = document.getElementById('freqValue');

  let ecgMode = false;

  function generateSignal(amp, freq) {
    const duration = 2, sr = 1000;
    const t = [], y = [];
    for (let i = 0; i < duration * sr; i++) {
      const ti = i / sr;
      t.push(ti);
      y.push(amp * Math.sin(2 * Math.PI * freq * ti));
    }
    return {t, y};
  }

  function generateECG() {
    const duration = 4, sr = 1000, hr = 1.2;
    const t = [], y = [];
    for (let i = 0; i < duration * sr; i++) {
      const ti = i / sr;
      t.push(ti);
      const ph = (ti * hr) % 1;
      let v = 0;
      // P wave
      if (ph > 0.10 && ph < 0.20) v += 0.15 * Math.sin(Math.PI*(ph-0.10)/0.10);
      // QRS complex
      if (ph > 0.25 && ph < 0.28) v -= 0.20 * Math.sin(Math.PI*(ph-0.25)/0.03);
      if (ph > 0.28 && ph < 0.32) v += 1.20 * Math.sin(Math.PI*(ph-0.28)/0.04);
      if (ph > 0.32 && ph < 0.35) v -= 0.30 * Math.sin(Math.PI*(ph-0.32)/0.03);
      // T wave
      if (ph > 0.40 && ph < 0.55) v += 0.30 * Math.sin(Math.PI*(ph-0.40)/0.15);
      y.push(v);
    }
    return {t, y};
  }

  function updatePlot() {
    if (ecgMode) {
      const sig = generateECG();
      const period = 1 / 1.2;
      const trace = {
        x: sig.t, y: sig.y, type: 'scatter', mode: 'lines',
        line: {color: '#27ae60', width: 2},
        hovertemplate: 'Time: %{x:.3f} s<br>%{y:.2f} mV<extra></extra>'
      };
      const layout = {
        title: {text: 'ECG — ~72 bpm (~1.2 Hz) — P-QRS-T complex', font: {size: 14, color: '#555'}},
        xaxis: {title: 'Time (seconds)', range: [0, 4], dtick: 0.5},
        yaxis: {title: 'Voltage (mV)', range: [-0.6, 1.5]},
        shapes: [{
          type: 'line', x0: 0, x1: period, y0: -0.45, y1: -0.45,
          line: {color: '#27ae60', width: 2}
        },{
          type: 'line', x0: 0, x1: 0, y0: -0.4, y1: -0.5,
          line: {color: '#27ae60', width: 2}
        },{
          type: 'line', x0: period, x1: period, y0: -0.4, y1: -0.5,
          line: {color: '#27ae60', width: 2}
        }],
        annotations: [
          {x: period/2, y: -0.55, text: '1 heartbeat = ' + (period*1000).toFixed(0) + ' ms',
           showarrow: false, font: {color: '#27ae60', size: 12}},
          {x: 0.15/1.2, y: 0.2, text: 'P', showarrow: true, ay: -25,
           font: {size: 11, color: '#888'}},
          {x: 0.30/1.2, y: 1.25, text: 'QRS', showarrow: true, ay: -20,
           font: {size: 11, color: '#888'}},
          {x: 0.47/1.2, y: 0.35, text: 'T', showarrow: true, ay: -25,
           font: {size: 11, color: '#888'}}
        ],
        margin: {t: 40, b: 50, l: 60, r: 20},
        plot_bgcolor: '#fafafa', paper_bgcolor: '#fafafa',
        hovermode: 'x unified'
      };
      Plotly.react('signalPlot', [trace], layout, {responsive: true, displayModeBar: false});
      return;
    }

    const amp = parseFloat(ampSlider.value);
    const freq = parseFloat(freqSlider.value);
    ampValue.textContent = amp.toFixed(1);
    freqValue.textContent = freq.toFixed(1);

    const sig = generateSignal(amp, freq);
    const period = 1 / freq;

    const trace = {
      x: sig.t, y: sig.y, type: 'scatter', mode: 'lines',
      line: {color: '#2980b9', width: 2.5}, name: 'Signal',
      hovertemplate: 'Time: %{x:.3f} s<br>Voltage: %{y:.1f} μV<extra></extra>'
    };

    const annotations = [], shapes = [];
    const peakT = 1 / (4 * freq);

    if (peakT < 2) {
      shapes.push({type:'line', x0:peakT, x1:peakT, y0:0, y1:amp,
        line:{color:'#e74c3c', width:2, dash:'dash'}});
      annotations.push({x:peakT, y:amp/2, xanchor:'left', yanchor:'middle',
        text:'  '+amp.toFixed(1)+' μV', showarrow:false,
        font:{color:'#e74c3c', size:13}});
    }

    if (freq <= 15 && period < 1.8) {
      shapes.push({type:'line', x0:0, x1:period, y0:-amp-1.2, y1:-amp-1.2,
        line:{color:'#27ae60', width:2}});
      shapes.push({type:'line', x0:0, x1:0, y0:-amp-0.8, y1:-amp-1.6,
        line:{color:'#27ae60', width:2}});
      shapes.push({type:'line', x0:period, x1:period, y0:-amp-0.8, y1:-amp-1.6,
        line:{color:'#27ae60', width:2}});
      annotations.push({x:period/2, y:-amp-2, xanchor:'center', yanchor:'top',
        text:'1 cycle = '+(period*1000).toFixed(0)+' ms', showarrow:false,
        font:{color:'#27ae60', size:12}});
    }

    const layout = {
      title: {text: freq.toFixed(1)+' Hz signal — '+amp.toFixed(1)+' μV amplitude',
        font:{size:14, color:'#555'}},
      xaxis: {title:'Time (seconds)', range:[0,2], dtick:0.5},
      yaxis: {title:'Voltage (μV)', range:[-12,12]},
      shapes: shapes, annotations: annotations,
      margin: {t:40, b:50, l:60, r:20},
      plot_bgcolor:'#fafafa', paper_bgcolor:'#fafafa',
      hovermode: 'x unified'
    };

    Plotly.react('signalPlot', [trace], layout, {responsive: true, displayModeBar: false});
  }

  window.showECGPreset = function() {
    ecgMode = true;
    document.getElementById('presetLabel').innerHTML =
      'ECG — ~72 bpm. Note: the y-axis switched to <b>millivolts</b> (1000× larger than EEG). Labels show the P wave, QRS complex, and T wave.';
    updatePlot();
  };

  window.setPreset = function(freq, amp, label) {
    ecgMode = false;
    freqSlider.value = freq;
    ampSlider.value = amp;
    document.getElementById('presetLabel').textContent = label;
    updatePlot();
  };

  ampSlider.addEventListener('input', function() {
    ecgMode = false;
    document.getElementById('presetLabel').textContent = '';
    updatePlot();
  });
  freqSlider.addEventListener('input', function() {
    ecgMode = false;
    document.getElementById('presetLabel').textContent = '';
    updatePlot();
  });

  // Initial render
  updatePlot();
})();
</script>
```

::: {.try-it}
**Experiment:**

- Set the frequency to **10 Hz**. This is roughly the speed of the **alpha rhythm** — the dominant EEG wave when you close your eyes.
- Now crank it to **1 Hz**. This is the **delta range** — slow waves you see in deep sleep.
- Set amplitude to **2 μV** — that's a weak EEG signal. Now set it to **10 μV** — stronger. In real EEG, amplitudes are typically 10–100 μV.
:::

## One More Thing: Phase

You now know amplitude (how tall) and frequency (how fast). There's one more property worth seeing — **phase**.

Phase tells you *where in the cycle* the wave is at a given moment. Two signals can have the same frequency and amplitude but be **offset** from each other — like two runners on the same track, one slightly ahead of the other.

Drag the slider below. Both waves have the same frequency and amplitude — only the phase changes.

```{=html}
<div class="widget-box">
  <div style="margin-bottom: 1em;">
    <label for="phaseSlider" style="font-weight:600;">Phase offset:
      <span id="phaseVal" style="color:#8e44ad; font-weight:700;">0°</span>
    </label><br>
    <input type="range" id="phaseSlider" min="0" max="360" step="5" value="0"
           style="width:100%; accent-color:#8e44ad;">
  </div>
  <div id="phasePlot" style="width:100%; height:280px;"></div>
  <div id="phaseCaption" style="font-size:0.88em; color:#555; margin-top:6px; font-style:italic;">
    Both waves are identical. Try shifting the purple wave by 180° — now they're perfectly out of sync.
  </div>
</div>

<script>
(function() {
  const sl = document.getElementById('phaseSlider');
  const valSpan = document.getElementById('phaseVal');

  function updatePhase() {
    const deg = parseFloat(sl.value);
    const rad = deg * Math.PI / 180;
    valSpan.textContent = deg + '°';

    const dur = 1.5, sr = 500, freq = 5, amp = 5;
    const t = [], y1 = [], y2 = [];
    for (let i = 0; i < dur * sr; i++) {
      const ti = i / sr;
      t.push(ti);
      y1.push(amp * Math.sin(2 * Math.PI * freq * ti));
      y2.push(amp * Math.sin(2 * Math.PI * freq * ti + rad));
    }

    const tr1 = {
      x: t, y: y1, type: 'scatter', mode: 'lines',
      line: {color: '#2980b9', width: 2.5}, name: 'Electrode A'
    };
    const tr2 = {
      x: t, y: y2, type: 'scatter', mode: 'lines',
      line: {color: '#8e44ad', width: 2.5, dash: 'dot'}, name: 'Electrode B (shifted)'
    };

    const layout = {
      xaxis: {title: 'Time (seconds)', range: [0, dur]},
      yaxis: {title: 'Voltage (μV)', range: [-7, 7]},
      margin: {t: 10, b: 45, l: 55, r: 15},
      plot_bgcolor: '#fafafa', paper_bgcolor: '#fafafa',
      legend: {x: 0.55, y: 1, bgcolor: 'rgba(255,255,255,0.8)', font: {size: 11}},
      hovermode: 'x unified'
    };

    Plotly.react('phasePlot', [tr1, tr2], layout, {responsive: true, displayModeBar: false});

    // Dynamic caption
    const cap = document.getElementById('phaseCaption');
    if (deg === 0) cap.textContent = 'Both waves are identical — they are "in phase."';
    else if (deg === 180) cap.textContent = 'Perfectly out of phase — when one goes up, the other goes down. If you added them, they\'d cancel out.';
    else if (deg === 90) cap.textContent = 'Quarter-cycle offset. One wave peaks exactly when the other crosses zero.';
    else if (deg === 360) cap.textContent = 'Full cycle shift — back to identical. 360° = 0°.';
    else cap.textContent = 'The purple wave is shifted by ' + deg + '° relative to the blue wave.';
  }

  sl.addEventListener('input', updatePhase);
  updatePhase();
})();
</script>
```

::: {.key-concept}
Phase won't come up much in this tutorial — but remember this slider. When we get to EEG localization later, neurologists use **phase reversal** (where one electrode's signal flips relative to its neighbor) to pinpoint exactly where a seizure starts. Same concept, real clinical stakes.
:::

## Why Does This Matter Clinically?

Different brain states produce different frequencies and amplitudes:

| Brain State | Dominant Frequency | Typical Amplitude | What's Happening |
|-------------|-------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| Deep sleep | 1–4 Hz (delta) | 100–200 μV | Large populations of neurons firing in sync |
| Relaxed, eyes closed | 8–13 Hz (alpha) | 30–50 μV | Thalamo-cortical idle rhythm |
| Focused attention | 13–30 Hz (beta) | 10–20 μV | Cortical neurons busy with different tasks |
| Active thinking | >30 Hz (gamma) | 5–10 μV | Local, fast processing |

Notice the pattern: **slower rhythms tend to be bigger, faster rhythms tend to be smaller.** This isn't a coincidence — slow rhythms reflect more neurons firing together (synchrony), which sums to a larger voltage at the scalp.

::: {.key-concept}
**Amplitude** tells you how much electrical activity is reaching the electrode.
**Frequency** tells you how fast that activity is oscillating.
Together, they are the first two things you assess when you look at any physiological recording — EEG, ECG, or EMG.
:::

## It's Not Just the Brain

The same two properties — amplitude and frequency — describe signals from other parts of the body:

| Signal | Typical Amplitude | Typical Frequency | Source |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|--------|
| **EEG** (brain) | 10–100 μV | 1–100 Hz | Cortical pyramidal neurons |
| **ECG** (heart) | 0.5–3 mV | ~1 Hz (60 bpm) | Cardiac muscle depolarization |
| **EMG** (muscle) | 0.1–5 mV | 20–500 Hz | Motor unit action potentials |

The ECG is about **1,000× larger** than the EEG. The EMG has **much higher frequencies** than either. These differences aren't trivia — they determine what equipment you need, what filters you set, and what artifacts you worry about. (More on artifacts in Module 6.)

## Compare: EEG vs ECG vs EMG

What do these three signals actually look like side by side? Same 2 seconds of time, three different parts of the body. Notice the **amplitude scales** — they're wildly different.

```{=html}
<div class="widget-box">
  <div id="stackedPlot" style="width:100%; height:600px;"></div>
  <div style="padding:10px 12px; background:#f0f0f0; border-radius:6px; margin-top:8px; font-size:0.9em; line-height:1.6;">
    <b style="color:#2980b9;">EEG</b> (top) — ~50 μV. Alpha rhythm at 10 Hz. This is what you see over the occipital cortex, eyes closed. Tiny signal.<br>
    <b style="color:#27ae60;">ECG</b> (middle) — ~1 mV. The tall spike is the QRS complex — ventricular depolarization. That's <b>1000× larger</b> than EEG.<br>
    <b style="color:#e74c3c;">EMG</b> (bottom) — bursts during contraction, quiet at rest. Much faster oscillations — motor units firing asynchronously.
  </div>
</div>

<script>
(function() {
  // Use seeded random for consistent display
  function seededRandom(seed) {
    let s = seed;
    return function() { s = (s * 16807) % 2147483647; return (s - 1) / 2147483646; };
  }

  const rand = seededRandom(42);
  const duration = 2, sr = 2000;
  const t = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < duration * sr; i++) t.push(i / sr);

  // EEG — alpha-dominant with some theta and beta
  const eeg = t.map(function(ti) {
    return 40 * Math.sin(2*Math.PI*10*ti + 0.3)
         + 12 * Math.sin(2*Math.PI*3.5*ti + 1.1)
         + 6 * Math.sin(2*Math.PI*21*ti + 0.7)
         + 10 * (rand() - 0.5);
  });

  // ECG — stylized but recognizable P-QRS-T
  const ecg = t.map(function(ti) {
    const hr = 1.2;
    const ph = (ti * hr) % 1;
    let v = 0;
    if (ph > 0.10 && ph < 0.20) v += 0.15 * Math.sin(Math.PI*(ph-0.10)/0.10);
    if (ph > 0.25 && ph < 0.28) v -= 0.20 * Math.sin(Math.PI*(ph-0.25)/0.03);
    if (ph > 0.28 && ph < 0.32) v += 1.20 * Math.sin(Math.PI*(ph-0.28)/0.04);
    if (ph > 0.32 && ph < 0.35) v -= 0.30 * Math.sin(Math.PI*(ph-0.32)/0.03);
    if (ph > 0.40 && ph < 0.55) v += 0.30 * Math.sin(Math.PI*(ph-0.40)/0.15);
    return v + 0.015 * (rand() - 0.5);
  });

  // EMG — burst in the middle
  const emg = t.map(function(ti) {
    const env = (ti > 0.5 && ti < 1.5) ? 1.5 * Math.sin(Math.PI*(ti-0.5)/1.0) : 0.03;
    let v = 0;
    for (let k = 1; k <= 8; k++) v += Math.sin(2*Math.PI*(40+k*35)*ti + k*1.7);
    return env * v / 5 + 0.03 * (rand() - 0.5);
  });

  const traceEEG = {
    x: t, y: eeg, type: 'scatter', mode: 'lines',
    line: {color: '#2980b9', width: 1.2}, name: 'EEG',
    xaxis: 'x', yaxis: 'y',
    hovertemplate: '%{y:.1f} μV<extra>EEG</extra>'
  };
  const traceECG = {
    x: t, y: ecg, type: 'scatter', mode: 'lines',
    line: {color: '#27ae60', width: 1.2}, name: 'ECG',
    xaxis: 'x2', yaxis: 'y2',
    hovertemplate: '%{y:.2f} mV<extra>ECG</extra>'
  };
  const traceEMG = {
    x: t, y: emg, type: 'scatter', mode: 'lines',
    line: {color: '#e74c3c', width: 1}, name: 'EMG',
    xaxis: 'x3', yaxis: 'y3',
    hovertemplate: '%{y:.2f} mV<extra>EMG</extra>'
  };

  const layout = {
    grid: {rows: 3, columns: 1, subplots: [['xy'],['x2y2'],['x3y3']], roworder: 'top to bottom'},
    xaxis:  {title: '', showticklabels: false, range: [0, 2]},
    xaxis2: {title: '', showticklabels: false, range: [0, 2]},
    xaxis3: {title: 'Time (seconds)', range: [0, 2]},
    yaxis:  {title: 'EEG (μV)', titlefont: {color: '#2980b9', size: 12}, range: [-80, 80]},
    yaxis2: {title: 'ECG (mV)', titlefont: {color: '#27ae60', size: 12}, range: [-0.8, 1.6]},
    yaxis3: {title: 'EMG (mV)', titlefont: {color: '#e74c3c', size: 12}, range: [-2.5, 2.5]},
    margin: {t: 15, b: 45, l: 65, r: 15},
    plot_bgcolor: '#fafafa', paper_bgcolor: '#fafafa',
    showlegend: false,
    hovermode: 'x unified'
  };

  Plotly.newPlot('stackedPlot', [traceEEG, traceECG, traceEMG], layout, {responsive: true, displayModeBar: false});
})();
</script>
```


## Quiz: Check Your Understanding

```{=html}
<div class="quiz-container">
  <h4>Quiz</h4>

  <div style="margin-bottom: 1.5em;" id="quiz1">
    <p><strong>Q1:</strong> A signal has a larger amplitude. What does that tell you?</p>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="q1" value="a"> A. It means the signal is faster
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="q1" value="b"> B. The voltage is higher — more electrical activity is reaching the electrode
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="q1" value="c"> C. The electrode is closer to the source
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="q1" value="d"> D. The patient is awake
    </label>
    <div id="q1feedback"></div>
  </div>

  <hr>

  <div style="margin-bottom: 1.5em;" id="quiz2">
    <p><strong>Q2:</strong> A healthy resting heart rate is about 70 beats per minute. What is the frequency of the ECG signal?</p>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="q2" value="a"> A. 0.5 Hz — about one beat every two seconds
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="q2" value="b"> B. About 1.2 Hz — roughly 70 beats per minute
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="q2" value="c"> C. 10 Hz — same as EEG alpha rhythm
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="q2" value="d"> D. 60 Hz — one beat per second
    </label>
    <div id="q2feedback"></div>
  </div>

  <hr>

  <div style="margin-bottom: 1em;" id="quiz3">
    <p><strong>Q3:</strong> Slow EEG rhythms (delta, during sleep) have much larger amplitude than fast rhythms (gamma). Why?</p>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="q3" value="a"> A. More neurons are firing at higher frequency
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="q3" value="b"> B. More neurons are firing in synchrony, so their voltages add up
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="q3" value="c"> C. The electrodes are more sensitive to slow signals
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="q3" value="d"> D. The skull filters out fast signals more than slow ones
    </label>
    <div id="q3feedback"></div>
  </div>
</div>

<script>
(function() {
  const answers = {
    q1: {correct: 'b', feedback: {
      a: ['incorrect', 'That would be frequency, not amplitude. Try again.'],
      b: ['correct', 'Correct. Amplitude = voltage magnitude. A bigger wave means more electrical activity is being picked up.'],
      c: ['incorrect', 'Electrode distance affects amplitude, but amplitude itself is a property of the signal — it tells you the voltage, not the distance.'],
      d: ['incorrect', 'Wakefulness can affect amplitude, but amplitude itself just describes how tall the wave is.']
    }},
    q2: {correct: 'b', feedback: {
      a: ['incorrect', 'Too slow — 0.5 Hz would mean 30 bpm. That\'s severe bradycardia.'],
      b: ['correct', 'Correct. 70 bpm ÷ 60 seconds = ~1.17 Hz. Each cardiac cycle takes about 0.85 seconds.'],
      c: ['incorrect', '10 Hz = 600 bpm. The patient would not be doing well.'],
      d: ['incorrect', 'Careful — 60 bpm would be 1 Hz, not 60 Hz. Hz = beats per <em>second</em>.']
    }},
    q3: {correct: 'b', feedback: {
      a: ['incorrect', 'It\'s not about individual neurons firing faster — it\'s about how many fire <em>together</em>.'],
      b: ['correct', 'Correct. Slow rhythms reflect large populations of neurons firing together. Like a stadium crowd doing a synchronized wave vs. everyone clapping at different times.'],
      c: ['incorrect', 'Electrodes don\'t have a frequency preference. The voltage difference comes from the brain itself.'],
      d: ['incorrect', 'The skull does attenuate signals, but that\'s not the main reason. Think about what the neurons are doing.']
    }}
  };

  ['q1','q2','q3'].forEach(function(qid) {
    const radios = document.querySelectorAll('input[name="' + qid + '"]');
    radios.forEach(function(r) {
      r.addEventListener('change', function() {
        const fb = answers[qid].feedback[r.value];
        const div = document.getElementById(qid + 'feedback');
        div.className = fb[0] === 'correct' ? 'quiz-feedback-correct' : 'quiz-feedback-incorrect';
        div.innerHTML = (fb[0] === 'correct' ? '✓ ' : '✗ ') + fb[1];
      });
    });
  });
})();
</script>
```

## Summary

You now know two things about any physiological signal:

1. **Amplitude** — how tall the wave is (measured in μV for EEG, mV for ECG/EMG)
2. **Frequency** — how many cycles per second (measured in Hz)

In the next module, we'll tackle a fundamental question: *how does a machine capture a continuously changing biological signal and turn it into numbers a computer can work with?*

(Spoiler: it can go wrong in interesting ways.)

::: {.module-nav}
[← Home](../index.qmd)
[Module 2: Sampling →](02_sampling.qmd)
:::
